submarines.name: USS Nautilus (SSN-571)
submarines.description: USS Nautilus was the world's first operational nuclear-powered submarine, commissioned in 1954. It demonstrated that nuclear propulsion could provide virtually unlimited range and endurance. In 1958, Nautilus made the first submerged transit of the geographic North Pole, earning the historic signal "Nautilus 90 North."
interestingFacts: First nuclear-powered submarine in the world
First vessel to transit the North Pole submerged (1958)
Powered by a Westinghouse S2W reactor
Now preserved as a museum ship in Groton, Connecticut

submarines.name: Typhoon Class (Project 941)
submarines.description: The Typhoon class is the largest submarine ever built. Six were constructed during the Cold War, each capable of carrying 20 R-39 Rif ballistic missiles with a range of 8,300 km. Their immense size allowed for unprecedented crew comfort, including a swimming pool, sauna, and bird aviary.
interestingFacts: Largest submarine ever constructed — 175 m long
Carried 20 ballistic missiles with up to 200 warheads total
Featured a swimming pool and sauna for crew comfort
Inspiration for the Red October in Tom Clancy's novel

submarines.name: Virginia Class (SSN-774)
submarines.description: The Virginia class is the US Navy's frontline nuclear attack submarine, designed for a wide range of open-ocean and littoral missions. Block V boats feature the Virginia Payload Module, adding 28 Tomahawk cruise missile capacity. Known for advanced quieting and sensor systems.
interestingFacts: Most advanced US attack submarine in service
Block V includes Virginia Payload Module with 28 Tomahawks
Designed for both open-ocean and shallow-water (littoral) operations
Features a photonics mast replacing traditional periscopes

submarines.name: Type 212A
submarines.description: The German Type 212A is regarded as one of the world's most advanced conventional submarines. Its fuel cell-based air-independent propulsion system allows submerged endurance measured in weeks, with near-zero acoustic signature. Used by Germany and Italy, and widely regarded as the benchmark for modern AIP submarine design.
interestingFacts: Uses hydrogen fuel cell AIP for exceptional stealth
Can remain submerged for weeks without surfacing
Among the quietest submarines ever built
Non-nuclear design considered a model for the next generation

submarines.name: HMS Dreadnought (S101)
submarines.description: The Dreadnought class will replace the UK's aging Vanguard-class SSBNs as the sole delivery platform for the UK's nuclear deterrent. Sharing a common pressure hull design with the SSN-AUKUS boats, the class will carry the Trident II D5 missile and feature a whole-life design philosophy for 30+ year service life.
interestingFacts: Will carry UK's Trident II D5 nuclear deterrent
Shares design elements with SSN-AUKUS programme
Planned for 30+ year operational service life

submarines.name: U-99 (Type VIIB)
submarines.description: U-99 was one of the most successful U-boats of World War II, commanded by ace commander Otto Kretschmer — Germany's most successful U-boat commander of the war. Operating primarily in the North Atlantic, U-99 sank 37 Allied ships. It was sunk in March 1941 after a convoy battle.
interestingFacts: Commanded by Otto Kretschmer, Germany's top U-boat ace
One of the most decorated U-boats of the Second World War
Sunk by HMS Walker and HMS Vanoc in March 1941

submarines.name: Sōryū Class
submarines.description: Japan's Sōryū-class submarines are among the most advanced conventional submarines in operation. Featuring Stirling engine AIP systems and, in later boats, lithium-ion batteries, they offer exceptional underwater endurance. The class is a key component of the Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force's Anti-Submarine Warfare capability.
interestingFacts: First submarines to use lithium-ion batteries operationally (last two boats)
Equipped with X-shaped stern planes for better manoeuvrability
One of the quietest diesel-electric submarines ever built
Japan has one of the world's most advanced conventional submarine fleets

submarines.name: Oscar II Class (Project 949A)
submarines.description: The Oscar II class is Russia's primary nuclear-powered cruise missile submarine (SSGN). Armed with 24 P-700 Granit anti-ship missiles, these submarines were designed to destroy NATO carrier battle groups. The Kursk was an Oscar II that sank in 2000 in a tragic accident that resulted in the loss of all 118 crew.
interestingFacts: Carries 24 P-700 Granit supersonic anti-ship cruise missiles
Designed specifically to destroy carrier battle groups
The Kursk (K-141) was an Oscar II, lost in 2000 with all 118 crew
Has a double hull design for improved survivability

explorerCards.title: US Navy Submarine Fleet
explorerCards.subtitle: ~68 Submarines
explorerCards.description: The United States operates the world's most capable submarine fleet — 14 Ohio-class SSBNs carrying Trident II missiles, and around 54 nuclear attack submarines of the Los Angeles, Seawolf, and Virginia classes.
facts: Largest nuclear submarine fleet by capability
14 Ohio-class SSBNs carry the US nuclear deterrent
Virginia class is being built at 2 per year
AUKUS will add Australian submarines to allied undersea force

explorerCards.title: Russian Navy Submarine Fleet
explorerCards.subtitle: ~64 Submarines
explorerCards.description: Russia operates the world's second-largest submarine fleet, including Borei-class SSBNs (the most advanced Russian ballistic missile submarines), Oscar II SSGNs, and a large force of Kilo-class diesel-electric boats.
facts: Borei class carries Bulava ballistic missiles
Kilo class is widely exported and has an excellent stealth reputation
Russia is developing the Yasen-M class nuclear attack submarines
Largest submarine fleet by total numbers

explorerCards.title: Chinese PLAN Submarine Fleet
explorerCards.subtitle: ~60+ Submarines
explorerCards.description: China's submarine force has grown dramatically in size and capability. The PLAN operates Jin-class SSBNs, Shang-class SSNs, and a large fleet of Type 039 Song and Kilo-class diesel-electric submarines.
facts: Fastest growing submarine fleet in the world
Jin-class SSBNs provide China's sea-based nuclear deterrent
Type 093B Shang-class SSNs are significantly quieter than predecessors
New Type 096 SSBN reportedly in development

explorerCards.title: The First Submarine: Hunley (1864)
explorerCards.subtitle: American Civil War
explorerCards.description: H.L. Hunley was the first submarine to sink an enemy warship in combat, attacking USS Housatonic on 17 February 1864. The hand-cranked vessel remains a landmark in naval history.
facts: First submarine to sink an enemy warship in combat
Propelled by 8 men turning a hand crank
Sank under mysterious circumstances after her attack
Raised in 2000 and now preserved in Charleston, SC

explorerCards.title: WWII: Battle of the Atlantic
explorerCards.description: The Battle of the Atlantic was the longest campaign of WWII. German U-boats sank over 3,500 Allied ships, while the Allies developed radar, depth charges, and convoy tactics to counter them.
facts: Longest continuous campaign of the Second World War
German U-boats sank over 3,500 Allied merchant and naval vessels
783 U-boats were lost with their crews
Breaking the Enigma cipher code was decisive in turning the tide

explorerCards.title: Cold War: The Silent War Under the Sea
explorerCards.description: The Cold War saw an unprecedented nuclear submarine arms race between the US and USSR — both sides deployed thousands of nuclear warheads aboard SSBN submarines as a second-strike deterrent.
facts: At peak, the USSR operated over 400 submarines
US and Soviet submarines routinely tracked each other in the deep ocean
Multiple close calls and accidents occurred, including Thresher and Scorpion losses
SOSUS underwater hydrophone network gave US decisive intelligence advantage

explorerCards.title: Pump-Jet Propulsion vs Propeller
explorerCards.subtitle: Engineering Comparison
explorerCards.description: Modern submarines increasingly use pump-jet propulsors instead of traditional propellers. Pump-jets are quieter and more efficient at high speeds, though heavier and more complex.
facts: Pump-jets significantly reduce cavitation noise
UK Astute class and US Virginia class use pump-jets
Traditional 7-blade skewed propellers are used on many modern boats
Pump-jet designs are classified — exact specifications are secret

explorerCards.title: Photonics Masts: No More Periscopes
explorerCards.subtitle: Modern Sensor Technology
explorerCards.description: Modern nuclear submarines replace the traditional optical periscope with non-hull-penetrating photonics masts — high-definition cameras mounted on retractable poles, with imagery relayed to displays throughout the boat.
facts: No physical hull penetration means simpler, stronger pressure hull
Multi-spectral cameras (visible, infrared, low-light) in one mast
Images displayed on multiple large screens in the control room
Operators no longer need to stand at a fixed periscope location

explorerCards.title: Largest Submarine: Typhoon Class
explorerCards.subtitle: 175 m — 48,000 tons submerged
explorerCards.description: The Soviet Typhoon class (Project 941) remains the largest submarine ever built. Six were constructed, each 175 metres long and displacing 48,000 tons submerged — larger than a World War II battleship.
facts: Length: 175 m (longer than 1.5 football fields)
Beam: 23 m wide
Submerged displacement: 48,000 tons
Crew quarters included a swimming pool, sauna, and bird aviary

explorerCards.title: Deepest Military Submarine: Soviet K-278 Komsomolets
explorerCards.subtitle: 1,000 m operating depth
explorerCards.description: The Soviet Project 685 Plavnik (NATO: Mike class) submarine K-278 Komsomolets could dive to 1,000 metres — deeper than any other military submarine before or since. Her titanium hull enabled this record.
facts: Maximum tested depth: 1,000 metres (record for military submarines)
Constructed entirely of titanium — extremely expensive
Only one was built before the programme was discontinued
Sank in 1989 due to fire, with loss of 42 crew members

explorerCards.title: Operation Ivy Bells: Cold War Wiretap
explorerCards.subtitle: US Intelligence Operation 1971–1981
explorerCards.description: US Navy submarines and divers placed listening devices on Soviet underwater communications cables in the Sea of Okhotsk — one of the most audacious intelligence operations of the Cold War.
facts: USS Halibut and USS Parche placed recording pods on Soviet cables
Recorded unencrypted Soviet military communications for a decade
Betrayed to the KGB by NSA employee Ronald Pelton in 1980
Parche received more unit commendations than any US warship

explorerCards.title: USS Triton: First Submerged Circumnavigation
explorerCards.subtitle: Operation Sandblast, 1960
explorerCards.description: USS Triton (SSRN-586) became the first vessel to circumnavigate the globe while remaining fully submerged — completing the 83-day voyage following Magellan's original route, in 1960.
facts: First and only fully-submerged circumnavigation of the globe
Completed 83,000 km in 83 days and 10 hours
Followed Ferdinand Magellan's 1519–1522 circumnavigation route
Only surfaced briefly to transfer a sick crew member
